title: Three different measures of labor costs
gist: Compares three labor-cost gauges — average hourly earnings, the Employment Cost Index (ECI), and unit labor cost (ULC) — explaining why average hourly earnings is distorted by composition effects and benefits exclusion, while ECI and ULC give cleaner reads, with ULC recently running below the others on strong productivity.

themes.theme: Average hourly earnings and its flaws

insights.claim: Average hourly earnings growth generally hovers around 2.5% but spiked sharply in early 2020 during the COVID-19 recession and has run between 3.5% and 4% in recent quarters.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Recent 3.5-4% wage growth sits above the long-run ~2.5% norm, a level the Fed watches for inflation persistence.
insights.locator: Average hourly wage of workers is the first and most obvious

insights.claim: Average hourly earnings is distorted by composition effects — St. Louis Fed research shows low-wage workers lose jobs faster in downturns, mechanically pushing measured average earnings up — and it excludes benefits like healthcare, pensions, and bonuses.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Treat wage-growth spikes during recessions skeptically — they can reflect who's left employed, not raises.

themes.theme: ECI and unit labor cost as cleaner gauges

insights.claim: The Employment Cost Index uses a fixed basket of labor to produce a pure cost change free of compositional shifts and includes both wages and benefits, so it trended downward during the COVID-19 recession unlike average hourly earnings.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: ECI is the more reliable compensation gauge through cycle turns; prefer it over average hourly earnings when reading labor-cost pressure.
insights.quote: The ECI uses a fixed ‘basket’ of labor to produce a pure cost change, free from the effects of workers moving between occupations and industries.
insights.locator: The Employment Cost Index (ECI) doesn't have these drawbacks

insights.claim: Unit labor cost measures hourly compensation relative to labor productivity, and recent ULC growth has fallen below the other measures because of solid labor productivity growth.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Soft ULC despite firm wage growth signals productivity is absorbing labor costs — disinflationary at the margin and supportive of margins.
insights.locator: Unit labor cost (ULC) is our third measure
